faq.q1: Hoe werkt de Decision Intelligence engine?

method.s1.label: 01 — SHADOW AI INFERENCE
method.s1.title: Detect redundancy without accessing your systems.
method.s1.p1c: : each tool maps to a functional category. Excess tools beyond the optimal threshold trigger redundancy cost weighted by √(N/50).
method.s1.threshnote: Above threshold = billable redundancy. Zylo 2024 + Flexera 2024.
method.s2.label: 02 — THE ENTROPY EQUATION
method.s2.desc: κ(N) models Brooks' Law for IT spend: coordination cost grows at
method.s2.descc: . The logarithm captures natural stabilization — 500 employees is not 10x more chaotic than 50.
method.entropy.zero: Compact org (≤50). Entropy is zero — one person governs all purchases.
method.entropy.low: Low entropy. Coordination manageable, but duplicates appear at team boundaries.
method.entropy.mod: Moderate entropy. Shadow IT and orphan licenses increasing. Monitoring recommended.
method.entropy.high: High entropy. Leaks near-inevitable without active FinOps governance. Audit urgent.
method.s3.label: 03 — ZERO-KNOWLEDGE PROTOCOL
method.s3.title: Your systems stay untouched.

vault.s5.title: The real questions. Direct answers.
vault.faq.q1: What data do you need for an audit?
vault.faq.a1: Billing exports (CSV/XLSX), license inventories, and optionally vendor invoices. We never need login credentials, API keys, OAuth access, or any connection to your production systems.
vault.faq.q2: Who can access my data inside Ghost Tax?
vault.faq.a2: Only the automated analysis engine processes your raw data. The assigned analyst reviews the structured output (anomalies, scores) — never the source files. All access is logged.
vault.faq.a3: We are SOC2 Type II audit-ready with all controls in place: encryption at rest/transit, RLS, audit logging, access controls, and 30-day auto-purge. Formal certification is in progress (target: Q3 2026). We list our actual controls rather than claiming certifications we don't yet hold.
vault.faq.q4: Can I delete my data before the 30-day window?
vault.faq.a4: Yes. Request deletion at security@ghost-tax.com and all data (raw files, processed results, reports) is permanently removed within 72 hours. You'll receive a deletion confirmation.
vault.faq.q5: What happens if there's a data breach?
vault.faq.a5: Our incident response protocol requires notification within 72 hours per US state breach notification laws. All data is encrypted at rest, so even in the unlikely event of unauthorized access, the data is unreadable without KMS keys.

faq.label: FREQUENTLY ASKED QUESTIONS
faq.title: Everything you need to know
faq.sub: Clear answers to common questions about Ghost Tax, our methodology, pricing, and security.
faq.cat1.title: PRODUCT & METHODOLOGY
faq.cat1.a1: Ghost Tax is a financial intelligence platform that detects hidden exposure in SaaS, AI, and Cloud spending. We use a 21-phase deterministic pipeline to analyze spending patterns, identify leakage, and deliver corrective protocols. No system access required — we work from public signals and user-declared data.
faq.cat1.a2: Every finding includes a confidence score (0-85). We separate observed signals from inferred and estimated ones. Our scores are conservative by design — we never overclaim. Typical accuracy improves significantly when combined with internal data intake in the Stabilization tier.
faq.cat1.q3: What types of exposure do you detect?
faq.cat1.a3: 12 types across three domains: SaaS (inactive licenses, duplicate tools, shadow IT, license tier misalignment), AI (ungoverned consumption, redundant models, unoptimized inference), and Cloud (idle resources, over-provisioning, commitment waste, egress inefficiency).
faq.cat2.title: PRICING & BILLING
faq.cat2.a1: We believe financial intelligence should be accessible. €490 is designed to be an impulse decision for any CFO or CIO — no procurement cycle, no vendor call, no demo. The typical detection finds 18-57x the cost in recoverable exposure.
faq.cat2.q2: What payment methods do you accept?
faq.cat2.a2: We accept all major credit cards, SEPA direct debit, and bank transfers for enterprise engagements. Payments are processed securely via Stripe. Invoicing available for Stabilization and Mission tiers.
faq.cat2.a3: If your detection reveals less than €5,000 in annual exposure, contact us within 14 days for a full refund. We stand behind our methodology.
faq.cat3.title: SECURITY & DATA
faq.cat3.q1: What data do you need from us?
faq.cat3.a1: For the free scan and Detection: only a company domain. No API access, no system agents, no credentials. For Stabilization and Mission tiers, we accept optional internal data (spending reports, vendor contracts) through a secure upload — but it's never required.
faq.cat3.a2: All data is stored in US-East-1 (Virginia) on SOC2-certified infrastructure. Data is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3). Raw analysis data is automatically purged after 30 days.
faq.cat3.q3: Do you sell or share my data?
faq.cat3.a3: Never. Your data is yours. We don't sell, share, or use client data for training models. Our privacy policy is clear and specific. See /legal/privacy for full details.
faq.cat4.title: GETTING STARTED
faq.cat4.a1: Go to the Decision Room (/intel), enter any company domain, and run a free scan. You'll see exposure diagnosis, proof architecture, and scenario simulation in minutes. No login required.
faq.cat4.q2: How long does it take to get results?
faq.cat4.a2: Free scan: 2-3 minutes. Paid Detection (€490): 48 hours for the complete Decision Pack. Stabilization Protocol: 5 business days. Monitoring: monthly reports delivered automatically.
faq.cat5.title: CFO & CTO OBJECTIONS
faq.cfo1.q: How is this different from our existing SaaS management tool?
faq.cfo1.a: SaaS management tools inventory what you have. Ghost Tax reveals what you're losing — the financial exposure from misaligned contracts, shadow AI, zombie licenses, and missed negotiation windows. We deliver a Decision Pack with EUR-quantified findings, not a dashboard you need to interpret.
faq.cfo2.q: What if you don't find significant savings?
faq.cfo2.a: In 200+ analyses, we've never found zero exposure. But if we don't identify at least 15% in addressable savings, you get a full refund. Our 30-day money-back guarantee has teeth.
faq.cfo3.q: Can I share the report with my board?
faq.cfo3.a: Yes — every Decision Pack includes a board-ready one-pager, CFO memo, CIO brief, and procurement action plan. They're designed to circulate internally and drive action.
faq.cfo4.q: How do you access our data?
faq.cfo4.a: We don't need access to your systems. Our analysis uses your domain and publicly available intelligence — vendor contracts, market benchmarks, and enrichment from 200+ SaaS vendors. Zero-knowledge protocol: we never touch your internal data.
faq.cfo5.q: Why €490 and not a free tool?
faq.cfo5.a: Free tools give you a dashboard. We give you a financial intelligence report with actionable EUR figures, negotiation playbooks, and a corrective protocol. The €490 typically pays for itself 7-15x in the first quarter of implementation.

faq.cta.sub: Run a free scan — results in minutes, no login required.

privacy.label: PRIVACY POLICY
privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.s1.title: Introduction
privacy.s1.content:
Ghost Tax Inc. ("Ghost Tax", "we", "us") operates the ghost-tax.com website and related services. This Privacy Policy explains how we collect, use, store, and protect your personal information when you use our services.

By using our services, you agree to the collection and use of information in accordance with this policy.
privacy.s2.title: Information We Collect
privacy.s2.content:
We collect information you provide directly:
• Email address (when you run a paid detection or submit a contact form)
• Company domain (when you use our analysis services)
• Company size and industry (optional, to improve analysis accuracy)
• Payment information (processed by Stripe — we never store card details)

We collect automatically:
• Usage data (pages visited, features used)
• Device information (browser type, operating system)
• IP address (for rate limiting and security)
privacy.s3.title: How We Use Your Information
privacy.s3.content:
We use your information to:
• Deliver financial exposure analysis and reports
• Process payments via Stripe
• Send delivery confirmations and follow-up communications
• Improve our analysis methodology and service quality
• Comply with legal obligations

We do NOT:
• Sell your data to third parties
• Share your data for advertising purposes
• Use your data to train AI models
• Profile you for purposes unrelated to our service
privacy.s4.title: Data Storage and Security
privacy.s4.content:
Your data is stored on SOC2-certified infrastructure in US-East-1 (Virginia). All data is encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3).

Raw analysis data is automatically purged after 30 days. Structured reports are retained for the duration of your business relationship with us.

We follow Zero-Knowledge audit principles: your data never touches our analysis engine in identifiable form.
privacy.s5.title: Sub-processors
privacy.s5.content:
We use the following third-party services:
• Supabase (database hosting, US) — SOC2 Type II
• Stripe (payment processing, US) — PCI DSS Level 1
• Vercel (website hosting, US) — SOC2 Type II
• Resend (email delivery, US) — SOC2 Type II
• OpenAI (analysis, US) — SOC2 Type II, no-logging mode
• Exa (data enrichment, US) — SOC2 Type II
privacy.s6.title: Your Rights (GDPR)
privacy.s6.content:
If you are located in the European Economic Area, you have the right to:
• Access your personal data
• Rectify inaccurate personal data
• Request erasure of your personal data
• Restrict processing of your personal data
• Data portability
• Object to processing

To exercise any of these rights, contact us at privacy@ghost-tax.com.
privacy.s7.title: Data Retention
privacy.s7.content:
• Raw analysis data: automatically deleted after 30 days
• Structured reports: retained while your account is active
• Payment records: retained for 7 years (legal requirement)
• Contact form submissions: retained for 2 years
• Email communications: retained for 2 years

You can request deletion at any time by contacting privacy@ghost-tax.com.
privacy.s8.title: Changes to This Policy
privacy.s8.content:
We may update this Privacy Policy from time to time. We will notify you of any changes by posting the new Privacy Policy on this page and updating the "Last updated" date.

We recommend reviewing this Privacy Policy periodically for any changes.
privacy.contact: Questions about this privacy policy? Contact us at privacy@ghost-tax.com.

mp.title: How the system detects, classifies, and bounds financial exposure.
mp.subtitle: This page explains what the engine observes, what it infers, what it estimates, and how confidence is formed. We publish our methodology, our limitations, and our boundaries.
mp.s1.label: 01 — WHAT THE SYSTEM OBSERVES
mp.s1.title: Direct signals from public sources and declared inputs.
mp.s1.p1: The system collects observable data from two channels: public web enrichment (via Exa neural search) and user-declared inputs.
mp.s1.pub1: Technology stack mentions (job postings, integrations pages, press)
mp.s1.pub2: Hiring velocity signals (open roles mentioning specific tools)
mp.s1.dec3: Monthly IT spend (optional — materially improves confidence)
mp.s1.p2: Observed signals carry the highest evidence weight. When the system can directly verify a technology mention from multiple public sources, that signal is classified as "observed."
mp.s2.label: 02 — WHAT THE SYSTEM INFERS
mp.s2.title: Structural patterns derived from the technology footprint.
mp.s2.p1: When the public enrichment reveals a company's technology stack, the system applies heuristic rules to detect structural patterns that commonly correlate with financial exposure.
mp.s2.r1d: Multiple overlapping AI tools (e.g., OpenAI + Anthropic + GitHub Copilot) suggest capability duplication.
mp.s2.r2d: Multiple monitoring/analytics platforms (e.g., Datadog + Amplitude) indicate feature overlap.
mp.s2.r3d: Enterprise-tier tools detected for organizations below 50 employees.
mp.s2.r4d: Multiple cloud providers suggest underutilized commitments.
mp.s2.r5d: Rapid hiring signals correlate with ungoverned tool adoption.
mp.s2.r6d: Large tool footprints carry statistically predictable inactive license rates.
mp.s2.r7d: Declared spend exceeding industry median per-employee benchmarks.
mp.s2.p2: Inferred signals are always labeled as such. They are never presented as observed facts. Each carries bounded impact ranges, not point estimates.
mp.s3.label: 03 — WHAT THE SYSTEM ESTIMATES
mp.s3.title: Financial projections from industry baselines.
mp.s3.p1: When signal-level data is insufficient, the system falls back to industry-calibrated baselines to produce bounded exposure estimates.
mp.s3.baselineText: 12–22% of annual IT spend is the typical "Ghost Tax" range for organizations with 50–500 employees. Source: Flexera 2024, Zylo 2024, Gartner 2025 composite.
mp.s3.whenText: When no monthly spend is declared, the system estimates it at ~380 EUR/employee/month. This is clearly marked as "estimated" and carries the lowest confidence tier.
mp.s3.p2: Estimated outputs always carry the lowest confidence scores and are explicitly separated from observed and inferred signals in the proof architecture.
mp.s4.label: 04 — CONFIDENCE MODEL
mp.s4.title: Numeric confidence, not qualitative labels.
mp.s4.p1: Every output carries a numeric confidence score from 0 to 100. This score is derived from four weighted inputs:
mp.s4.f1d: More public signals = higher confidence in technology footprint.
mp.s4.f2d: Similar historical cases in the knowledge base improve accuracy.
mp.s4.f4d: User-provided spend data materially improves exposure accuracy.
mp.s4.p2: The system never claims 100/100 confidence. Maximum is capped at 85. Below 30, results include an explicit limitation warning.
mp.s5.label: 05 — BOUNDARIES AND CAVEATS
mp.s5.title: What the system does not do.
mp.s5.b1: Does not access internal billing systems, ERP, or vendor APIs.
mp.s5.b2: Does not read contracts, invoices, or utilization logs.
mp.s5.b3: Does not perform real-time monitoring or continuous scanning.
mp.s5.b4: Does not provide department-level or per-user attribution.
mp.s5.b5: Does not use neural networks or ML models — all detection is deterministic and heuristic.
mp.s5.b6: Cannot detect exposure patterns that leave no public signal.
mp.s5.b7: Actual exposure may differ from estimates — ranges reflect structural uncertainty.
mp.s5.p1: These limitations are displayed in the analysis output itself, not hidden in fine print.
mp.s6.label: 06 — WHY THE OUTPUT IS DECISION-USEFUL
mp.s6.title: Useful without internal ledger access. More useful with it.
mp.s6.p1: The public/self-serve analysis works from publicly available signals and optional declared inputs. This is sufficient to:
mp.s6.c1: Identify the likely shape and magnitude of financial exposure.
mp.s6.c2: Classify signals by evidence tier so the buyer knows what is proven vs projected.
mp.s6.c3: Produce bounded ranges that are directionally reliable for budget conversations.
mp.s6.c4: Generate stakeholder memos that frame the case for internal circulation.
mp.s6.c5: Create competitive pressure via peer benchmarking (when data is sufficient).
mp.s6.c6: Quantify the cost of inaction through loss velocity.
mp.s7.label: 07 — WHAT DEEPENS IN THE PAID PROTOCOL
mp.s7.title: Internal data intake unlocks precision the public analysis cannot reach.
mp.s7.p1: The paid Detection Protocol ($490 / €490) adds a structured data intake phase where the organization provides billing exports, license inventories, and vendor contracts. This enables:
mp.s7.d1d: Specific renegotiation, downgrade, and consolidation recommendations per vendor.
mp.s7.d2d: Inactive and underutilized seats identified with exact counts.
mp.s7.d3d: Renewal dates, auto-renewal clauses, and negotiation windows mapped.
mp.s7.d4d: Sequenced action plan with owner assignment and timeline.
mp.s7.p2: The paid protocol does not replace the public analysis — it deepens it. Confidence scores increase materially when internal data is available.

ghosttax.faq.a2: According to Gartner, Flexera, and industry analyses, mid-market companies (50-1000 employees) typically waste 18-32% of their total IT spend. For a company spending EUR 120,000/month on IT, that is EUR 259,200 to EUR 460,800 per year in invisible waste.
ghosttax.faq.q3: What causes IT budget waste in companies?
ghosttax.faq.a3: The five main sources of IT waste are: (1) unused or underused SaaS licenses, (2) shadow IT and unauthorized AI tool subscriptions, (3) over-provisioned cloud infrastructure, (4) redundant tools with overlapping functionality, and (5) auto-renewed contracts at unfavorable terms.

ib.saas.label: INTELLIGENCE BENCHMARK
ib.saas.title: SaaS & AI Cost Exposure by Industry
ib.saas.subtitle: Per-employee annual exposure benchmarks across 8 industries. Median represents typical waste; top-quartile represents realistic optimization targets. Source: Flexera 2024, Zylo 2024, Gartner 2025 composite.
ib.saas.tableNote: Gap = addressable waste per employee if the organization moved from median to top-quartile performance. For a 200-person company in Financial Services, this represents ~220k EUR/yr in recoverable spend.
ib.saas.finding1: Financial Services shows the highest per-employee exposure (2,200 EUR/yr median) due to regulatory-driven tool proliferation and multi-vendor compliance requirements.
ib.saas.finding2: Technology / SaaS companies are second (1,800 EUR/yr) driven by AI tool redundancy and engineering-led tool adoption without procurement oversight.
ib.saas.finding3: Manufacturing shows the lowest exposure (1,200 EUR/yr) but the widest gap between median and top-quartile, suggesting high variance in IT governance maturity.
ib.saas.finding4: AI-specific exposure is growing fastest across all sectors — organizations with 2+ overlapping AI tools show 15–30% higher per-employee waste.
ib.saas.finding5: Shadow IT (ungoverned tool adoption) accounts for an estimated 25–40% of total exposure in high-growth companies.
ib.saas.faq1.a: SaaS cost exposure is the portion of annual software spend that delivers no value — idle licenses, duplicate tools, oversized tiers, and unmanaged subscriptions. Industry data shows 12–22% of total SaaS spend falls into this category.
ib.saas.faq2.a: Total annual SaaS/AI/cloud waste divided by headcount. This normalizes for company size and allows cross-industry comparison. Benchmarks are derived from Flexera, Zylo, and Gartner composite data.
ib.saas.faq3.q: What is the difference between median and top-quartile exposure?
ib.saas.faq3.a: Median represents the typical exposure for organizations in a given industry. Top-quartile represents the exposure level of the best-managed 25% — this is the realistic optimization target.
ib.saas.faq4.a: Yes. Organizations with 2+ AI tools (ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude, etc.) show 15–30% higher per-employee exposure due to capability overlap, ungoverned experimentation, and duplicate inference costs.
ib.saas.faq5.q: How can I benchmark my organization?
ib.saas.faq5.a: Run a free detection in the Ghost Tax Decision Room. The system will calculate your exposure, compare you to industry peers, and show your percentile position — no payment or integration required.
ib.saas.ctaDesc: Run a free detection to benchmark against your industry peers.
ib.saas.ctaNote: No payment required. Domain-only input. Results in seconds.
ib.shadow.label: INTELLIGENCE BENCHMARK
ib.shadow.title: Shadow AI Governance
ib.shadow.subtitle: How ungoverned AI adoption creates financial exposure. Detection patterns, cost impact data, and practical governance frameworks.
ib.shadow.pat1.name: AI Tool Redundancy
ib.shadow.pat1.desc: Multiple AI assistants (ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude, Gemini) active simultaneously across teams with overlapping capabilities.
ib.shadow.pat1.detect: Public tech stack analysis reveals 2+ AI tools with overlapping use cases.
ib.shadow.pat2.name: Ungoverned Experimentation
ib.shadow.pat2.desc: Individual teams adopting AI tools via personal credit cards or team-level subscriptions outside procurement.
ib.shadow.pat2.detect: Hiring signals + tool mentions in job postings indicate rapid, ungoverned adoption.
ib.shadow.pat3.name: Inference Cost Sprawl
ib.shadow.pat3.desc: API-based AI usage (OpenAI, Anthropic APIs) without consumption governance, leading to unpredictable and growing costs.
ib.shadow.pat3.detect: Multiple API provider references in public documentation or engineering blogs.
ib.shadow.pat4.name: Model-to-Task Mismatch
ib.shadow.pat4.desc: Using expensive frontier models (GPT-4, Claude Opus) for tasks that smaller, cheaper models handle equally well.
ib.shadow.pat4.detect: Inferred from AI tool footprint size and engineering team growth signals.
ib.shadow.gov1.title: Procurement Gate
ib.shadow.gov1.desc: Require approval for AI tool purchases above 50 EUR/mo per user. This catches 80% of shadow subscriptions without creating excessive friction.
ib.shadow.gov2.title: Approved Tool Registry
ib.shadow.gov2.desc: Maintain a curated list of sanctioned AI tools by category (code assistance, content, analytics, automation). Teams pick from the list instead of discovering independently.
ib.shadow.gov3.title: Quarterly Utilization Review
ib.shadow.gov3.desc: Review active AI subscriptions quarterly. Identify tools with <30% utilization or >50% capability overlap with another tool. Consolidate or cancel.
ib.shadow.gov4.title: Cost-Per-Outcome Tracking
ib.shadow.gov4.desc: Shift from cost-per-seat to cost-per-outcome. A 20 USD/seat AI tool that saves 4 hours/week is well-governed. A 20 USD/seat tool with 10% adoption is shadow waste.
ib.shadow.faq1.a: Shadow AI refers to AI tools and services adopted by employees or teams without formal IT procurement approval. This includes personal ChatGPT subscriptions, unapproved API integrations, and team-level AI tool purchases that bypass governance workflows.
ib.shadow.faq2.q: How much does shadow AI cost a typical organization?
ib.shadow.faq2.a: For a 200-person technology company, shadow AI typically adds 2,000–18,000 EUR/month in unmanaged spend. The cost is compounded by redundancy — multiple teams often pay for overlapping AI capabilities independently.
ib.shadow.faq3.q: How do you detect shadow AI without agent installation?
ib.shadow.faq3.a: Ghost Tax detects shadow AI through public signal analysis: job postings mentioning AI tools, tech stack disclosures, hiring velocity patterns, and industry-calibrated heuristics. No internal system access is required at the detection stage.
ib.shadow.faq4.q: What governance framework reduces shadow AI exposure?
ib.shadow.faq4.a: Effective shadow AI governance requires three components: (1) a lightweight procurement gate for AI tool purchases above a threshold, (2) an approved AI tool registry with clear category coverage, and (3) periodic utilization review to identify and consolidate redundant subscriptions.
ib.shadow.ctaDesc: Enter your domain. The system detects AI tool redundancy and governance gaps automatically.
ib.cfo.label: CFO INTELLIGENCE
ib.cfo.title: CFO Guide to Technology Spend Exposure
ib.cfo.subtitle: What finance leaders need to know about hidden SaaS, AI, and cloud costs — and how to build a detection-to-correction pipeline without a six-month implementation.
ib.cfo.gapIntro: Most CFOs have accurate visibility into headcount, real estate, and capital expenditure. Technology spend — particularly SaaS, AI tools, and cloud services — remains structurally opaque for three reasons:
ib.cfo.gap1.title: Decentralized purchasing
ib.cfo.gap1.desc: Engineering, marketing, and product teams purchase tools independently. Many subscriptions sit below approval thresholds and never enter centralized reporting.
ib.cfo.gap2.title: Consumption-based pricing
ib.cfo.gap2.desc: Cloud and AI costs are usage-based, making them unpredictable and invisible until invoice time. Committed capacity often goes underutilized.
ib.cfo.gap3.title: Category sprawl
ib.cfo.gap3.desc: The average mid-market company uses 100–300 SaaS tools. No single system tracks them all. Shadow IT accounts for 25–40% of the total in high-growth organizations.
ib.cfo.cat1.name: 1. Redundant Tools
ib.cfo.cat1.desc: Multiple tools serving the same function across teams. Most common in monitoring, AI, project management, and communication.
ib.cfo.cat2.name: 2. Idle Licenses
ib.cfo.cat2.desc: Paid seats with zero or minimal usage. Often persists because no one owns deprovisioning.
ib.cfo.cat3.name: 3. Oversized Plans
ib.cfo.cat3.desc: Enterprise tiers purchased for teams that need only standard features. Vendor upselling is the primary driver.
ib.cfo.cat4.name: 4. Shadow Subscriptions
ib.cfo.cat4.desc: Tools purchased outside procurement — personal credit cards, team-level accounts, free-tier upgrades.
ib.cfo.cat5.name: 5. Cloud Commitment Waste
ib.cfo.cat5.desc: Reserved instances, committed-use discounts, and savings plans that are underutilized or misallocated.
ib.cfo.detIntro: Traditional SaaS management platforms require SSO integration, agent deployment, and months of onboarding. External signal analysis provides a faster first pass:
ib.cfo.det1.output: Directional exposure estimate, signal classification, peer benchmark
ib.cfo.det2.output: Prioritized actions, ownership mapping, payback projections
ib.cfo.det4.output: Continuous monitoring, automated optimization, governance workflows
ib.cfo.faq1.q: How much technology spend is typically wasted?
ib.cfo.faq1.a: Industry data consistently shows 12–22% of annual SaaS, AI, and cloud spend is wasted through idle licenses, redundant tools, oversized plans, and ungoverned adoption. For a company spending 500k EUR/yr on IT, this represents 60k–110k EUR in addressable waste.
ib.cfo.faq2.q: Why doesn't our ERP catch this?
ib.cfo.faq2.a: ERP systems track committed costs, not consumption efficiency. They report what was purchased, not what is actually used. SaaS sprawl, shadow AI, and cloud commitment waste exist in the gap between committed spend and realized value — a gap ERPs are not designed to measure.
ib.cfo.faq3.q: What is the fastest way to detect exposure?
ib.cfo.faq3.a: External signal analysis can identify exposure patterns in minutes using public data: technology stack footprint, hiring signals, and industry benchmarks. This provides a directional estimate without requiring internal system access. Declared spend data improves accuracy significantly.
ib.cfo.faq4.q: What does a corrective protocol deliver?
ib.cfo.faq4.a: A structured corrective protocol delivers: (1) prioritized list of corrective actions, (2) ownership mapping for each action, (3) estimated savings per action with payback timeline, (4) vendor-specific remediation guidance, and (5) executive-ready decision pack for internal distribution.
ib.cfo.faq5.a: Quick wins (license downgrades, obvious redundancy removal) can be executed in 30 days. Structured optimization typically shows results in 60–90 days. Full corrective protocols with governance hardening take 3–6 months but deliver sustained, compounding savings.

caseStudies.aggregate.label: AGGREGATE ACROSS 3 CASES
caseStudies.case1.headline: Fintech scale-up: 340k EUR/yr in redundant SaaS and Shadow AI detected in 48h
caseStudies.case1.challenge: Series B fintech, 400 employees, 3 offices. Rapid headcount growth from 90 to 400 in 18 months had generated tool sprawl. No central SaaS inventory. Finance estimated 180k EUR/yr in software spend but had no line-item visibility beyond top 5 vendors. Shadow AI adoption across engineering and product teams was untracked.
caseStudies.case1.detection: Ghost Tax pipeline identified 23 active SaaS tools from public signals and declared spend data. 12 of 23 showed usage patterns inconsistent with seat count. AI tool redundancy flagged across 4 categories (writing, coding, data analysis, meeting notes). Exa enrichment cross-referenced 200+ vendor pricing baselines. Confidence score: 71/100 (strong).
caseStudies.case1.outcome: Decision Pack delivered in 31 hours. CFO memo quantified 340k EUR/yr exposure (range: 290k-390k). Tool consolidation roadmap reduced active vendors from 23 to 11. AI governance policy implemented. Actual savings realized at 9-month mark: 310k EUR/yr. ROI on audit: 7x in year one.
caseStudies.case1.quote: The CFO memo landed in my inbox before I expected it. The board one-pager was shared at the next governance meeting without any preparation on our side. That kind of decision velocity is what we needed.
caseStudies.case1.quoteRole: VP Finance, Fintech Scale-up, Western Europe
caseStudies.case2.headline: German manufacturer: 270k EUR/yr AI and cloud overspend identified across 6 business units
caseStudies.case2.challenge: 1,200-employee manufacturer with 6 autonomous business units. Each unit had independently procured cloud and AI tooling without central oversight. Group IT estimated 600k EUR/yr in cloud and AI spend but variance between units was opaque. Two Microsoft EA renewals pending within 90 days — no negotiation leverage identified.
caseStudies.case2.detection: Pipeline detected 180k EUR/yr in AI overspend from tool redundancy and idle compute. Renewal Sniper plugin flagged both Microsoft EA windows with 60 and 82 days remaining. Benchmark engine placed the company at 74th percentile for cloud spend per employee in manufacturing — 28 points above industry median. Confidence score: 68/100 (strong).
caseStudies.case2.outcome: Negotiation playbook delivered for both Microsoft renewals. Combined negotiated discount: 90k EUR over 3 years. AI consolidation across 6 units reduced redundant tooling spend by 180k EUR/yr. Group IT now has a unified vendor governance policy. Total exposure addressed: 270k EUR/yr.
caseStudies.case2.quote: We had the right data at exactly the right moment before renewal. The negotiation playbook gave our procurement lead a clear script — we got a 22% discount we wouldn't have known to ask for.
caseStudies.case3.headline: French consultancy: 95k EUR/yr in zombie licenses recovered, board action same day
caseStudies.case3.challenge: 250-employee consulting firm, Paris. Recent merger had doubled headcount but not rationalized the software stack. Both legacy stacks remained active. Finance was paying for 250+ seats on tools with fewer than 80 active users. No usage data available — IT had lost track of what was provisioned for acquired staff.
caseStudies.case3.detection: Ghost Tax identified 14 tools with significant seat-to-user mismatch from public enrichment and declared headcount data. License waste module calculated 95k EUR/yr in zombie license exposure (range: 78k-112k). Delivery time: 48 hours from domain submission. Confidence score: 64/100 (strong).
caseStudies.case3.outcome: Board one-pager presented at same-day executive committee meeting. IT rationalization mandate issued. 14 tools reviewed, 6 licenses renegotiated or terminated within 30 days. 95k EUR/yr savings confirmed at 6-month audit. Procurement lead promoted the Ghost Tax approach as the standard for future M&A integration.
caseStudies.case3.quote: We used the board one-pager in the exec committee meeting the same afternoon it arrived. The numbers were precise enough to mandate action, and the corrective path was already mapped.
